metrics.justification: [Mock justification] The plan demonstrates strong DRY adherence. Key observations: (1) Shared configurations properly abstracted - CaseScore format defined once, Haiku model specified as standard, tools consolidated in single task T-3.1. (2) Common requirements consolidated into AC-014 (agent format) and AC-015 (tools) rather than repeated per-agent. (3) The 12 parallel judge conversion tasks (T-2.1 to T-2.12) are not DRY violations - each produces a distinct agent with unique evaluation criteria from different Jinja2 templates. (4) Cross-repo dependencies structured with single definitions linked via neededBy arrays. Minor deductions: (a) Acceptance criteria AC-001 through AC-012 repeat 'with Haiku model and CaseScore output' phrase that could be abstracted (-0.03). (b) Some task descriptions include '[CROSS-REPO: agent-evaluation-framework]' prefix that duplicates information in crossRepoDependencies section (-0.02). (c) Gap documentation slightly overlaps with PRD content (-0.03). Overall, the plan correctly identifies shared utilities and avoids duplicated logic while maintaining clear, traceable documentation.

[Mock justification] The plan demonstrates strong SSOT adherence: (1) CaseScore schema is defined centrally in PRD and referenced consistently; (2) State ownership is clear - judges are stateless, orchestrator owns aggregation; (3) Configuration values (model: haiku, tools) are applied uniformly without conflicting definitions; (4) Cross-repo dependencies track source templates without duplicating them; (5) Each judge is an independent unit with clear responsibility. Minor deduction: Each agent will inline CaseScore format instructions rather than referencing a centralized definition, but this is acceptable for stateless evaluation agents to remain self-contained and reduce coupling. No data integrity risks identified.

[Mock justification] The implementation plan demonstrates excellent simplicity and directness. POSITIVE FACTORS: (1) Straightforward 1:1 conversion approach - each Jinja2 template becomes one markdown agent file with no intermediate abstractions or shared base classes. (2) No over-engineered patterns - the plan avoids creating unnecessary factories, builders, or inheritance hierarchies for what is essentially a file conversion task. (3) Flat directory structure - all 12 judges go in a single judges/ directory without premature sub-categorization (e.g., no solid/, response-quality/, etc. subdirectories). (4) Simple input/output contract - agents accept text inputs and return CaseScore JSON, with no complex state management or inter-agent communication. (5) Read-only tools only (Glob, Grep, Read, LS) - appropriate minimal toolset for evaluation tasks. (6) No premature optimization - explicitly defers orchestration, aggregation, and testing infrastructure as out of scope per PRD. MINOR COMPLEXITY CONCERNS: (1) The cross-repo dependency tracking section adds metadata that could be considered overkill for a simple conversion task, though it documents legitimate integration concerns. (2) The gaps section identifies inconsistencies (12 vs 13 judges) that don't affect implementation complexity. OVERALL: The plan maintains focus on the core task - converting templates to agent definitions - without introducing unnecessary architectural complexity. Each task is atomic, self-contained, and directly traceable to acceptance criteria.
